summary: DHH argues that social media platforms have engineered away all friction from human interaction, creating a toxic environment where every fleeting impulse gets broadcast directly into others' consciousness. After hitting a personal breaking point on Twitter in April 2021, he quit engaging with strangers there and replaced it with friction-mediated connections: email newsletters and open source collaboration on GitHub. He found that the friction inherent in these older internet formats serves as a high-grade filter, producing authentic, good-faith exchanges. He questions whether a social media platform designed around friction could ever compete with frictionless alternatives, while concluding that friction is essential for healthy human connection.
key_points: Social media has systematically removed all friction between emotional impulse and public expression, creating a toxic feedback loop that degrades discourse
After quitting Twitter engagement, DHH found that email and open source collaboration restore faith in stranger-to-stranger connection because friction filters out the frivolous and malevolent
Friction is a feature, not a bug — historically, the effort required to contact someone served as a natural quality filter on communication
Tinder's two-way matching and HEY's Screener are examples of products that embrace friction as a design principle
Content moderation and fact-checking have failed as solutions; the answer lies in redesigning how connections are initiated
money_quotes: The distance from emotion to reaction has been whittled away one A/B test at the time, and there's barely any left.
We need others enough that even if they become these warped beings, pulled apart by the black hole, they're still preferable to the cold emptiness of being alone.
Giving voice to every fleeting emotion or thought is a pathology. Listening to all these voices sure to eventually develop into one.
Whittling it all away such that every little impulse of every little person is connected to an outlet of expression is perhaps the worst crime committed by the modern internet.
Democratizing the power to force your way into someone's head is intoxicating.
We really are all better off when creating a connection to another human being is gated by a modicum of friction.
Work can unite what politics so often divide.
key_insight: Friction in communication is not an obstacle to overcome but a vital filter that produces authentic human connection, and social media's relentless removal of it has degraded public discourse.
